skill.name: when-validating-code-works-use-functionality-audit
skill.description: Validates whether generated or modified code works by setting up test environments, running realistic tests, debugging failures, and reporting readiness.
skill.summary: Run execution-based functionality audits for generated or changed code.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and environment access patterns. Review confirms the main risk is legitimate but powerful testing workflow behavior, not confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection. Publish with a warning that the skill may run local commands, install packages, create temporary files, and store test artifacts.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution Required by Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users or agents to run npx, npm, Python, grep, diff, jq, and related shell commands. This is central to functionality testing, but it can execute project code and install packages, so users should run it only in trusted repositories and isolated environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections directly contain shell commands used by the audit workflow. The commands are expected for the skill purpose, but they still create operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes and Cleanup in Temporary Sandboxes
medium_findings.description: The workflow creates temporary directories, writes configuration and test files, reads source files, and includes a cleanup command for a fixed /tmp audit path. These operations are not malicious, but mistakes in path handling could overwrite or delete local artifacts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show concrete filesystem operations and a scoped recursive delete under /tmp. The scope reduces severity, but the operations remain meaningful.

medium_findings.title: Test Artifacts May Persist Sensitive Project Output
medium_findings.description: The skill stores configuration, execution results, logs, and artifact paths in Claude Flow memory. There is no evidence of exfiltration, but test logs can contain application data, stack traces, or secrets if the audited project emits them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The memory-store behavior is explicit, but whether sensitive data is captured depends on the project under test. This warrants a publication warning rather than a block.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reviewed examples and diagram lines do not implement cryptographic algorithms. Static detections appear to match ordinary documentation, labels, or example strings rather than MD5, SHA1, or other weak crypto use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe audit phases, metadata, or error categorization. No cryptographic API use or hashing implementation is present there.

low_findings.title: Documented URLs Do Not Show Data Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs in the README point to public Claude Flow documentation, issues, and discussions. No reviewed line sends local files, environment values, or test data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are support links in documentation. I found no evidence that the skill transmits data to them.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Signals Are Example Import Paths
low_findings.description: The static path traversal matches occur in generated test examples that use relative imports such as parent-directory module references. They are not used to read arbitrary user-supplied paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside test templates and use conventional relative imports. No attacker-controlled path construction is shown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Test Orchestration
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow depends on shell execution for setup, test execution, log parsing, and report generation. This is expected for a testing skill but should be constrained to disposable workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and semantically tied to the skill purpose. Risk comes from execution context, not hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment File Example Uses Placeholder Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: The .env example uses test-only values such as API_KEY=test-key-12345 and DATABASE_URL=sqlite::memory:. It demonstrates test setup and does not read real credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The values are visibly placeholders for a local test environment. The concern would increase only if users copied real secrets into generated artifacts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Scoped Recursive Delete in Troubleshooting Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The README includes rm -rf for /tmp/audit-sandbox as a cleanup step. The path is fixed and scoped to a temporary audit directory, but recursive deletion commands can be destructive if modified incorrectly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is real but limited to /tmp/audit-sandbox. It is a warning-level operational risk, not evidence of malicious root or home deletion.

content.user_title: Validate Code Functionality With Runtime Audits
content.value_statement: Generated code can look complete while failing during execution. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through sandbox setup, realistic tests, debugging, and readiness reporting.
actual_capabilities: Creates a structured five-phase workflow for execution-based code validation.
Guides setup of temporary test environments for Node.js and Python projects.
Runs tests with coverage collection and captures execution output.
Categorizes failures into syntax, runtime, logic, dependency, and integration issues.
Defines validation gates for regressions, coverage, and production readiness.
Produces stakeholder-facing audit reports and metrics summaries.
limitations: Requires local command execution and access to project files.
Depends on available test frameworks and project dependencies.
Does not prove security correctness or complete business requirements coverage.
May need manual review when tests require external services or secrets.

use_cases.title: Validate AI-generated modules
use_cases.description: Run generated code in a controlled test workflow before merging it into a production branch.

use_cases.title: Check risky refactors
use_cases.description: Use execution evidence, regression checks, and coverage metrics to decide whether a refactor is ready.

use_cases.title: Prepare release quality gates
use_cases.description: Create repeatable functionality reports that summarize failures, fixes, readiness, and remaining risks.

prompt_templates.title: Basic functionality check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner validation after a small code change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to validate the changed code. Set up a temporary test environment, run the relevant tests, and summarize whether the feature works.

prompt_templates.title: Debug failing behavior
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow for a known failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this failing feature with realistic inputs. Capture the error output, identify the root cause, apply a minimal fix, and rerun the affected tests.

prompt_templates.title: Regression-focused audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced validation before merging a refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a functionality audit for this refactor. Compare baseline and retest results, flag regressions, and report coverage and readiness status.

prompt_templates.title: Release readiness assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a full five-phase functionality audit. Include sandbox setup, realistic test execution, debugging, validation gates, and a final production readiness recommendation.

output_examples.input: Validate a modified login flow before merge.
output_examples.output: The audit reports that authentication tests passed after one validation fix. Coverage stayed above the threshold, and no regressions were detected.

output_examples.input: Check a data processing refactor for runtime issues.
output: The fix was validated with reruns.
The final report recommends adding one missing boundary test.

output_examples.input: Assess whether an API endpoint is ready for release.
output_examples.output: The readiness summary lists passing endpoint tests, coverage status, remaining integration concerns, and a clear release recommendation.

best_practices: Run the workflow in a disposable sandbox or container when testing unfamiliar code.
Use realistic inputs and include edge cases before accepting the audit result.
Review generated logs before storing or sharing them outside the project.
anti_patterns: Do not run dependency installation commands in sensitive repositories without review.
Do not treat passing generated tests as proof of complete product correctness.
Do not include real production secrets in temporary test environment files.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed for runtime validation and may run tests, package commands, and project scripts.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use a disposable sandbox or container for untrusted code because the workflow can execute local commands.

faq.question: Does it replace code review?
faq.answer: No. It validates behavior through execution, but reviewers still need to inspect design, security, and maintainability.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it work without existing tests?
faq.answer: It can guide creation of basic tests, but strong results require meaningful project-specific test cases.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill legitimately uses commands and filesystems. No malicious intent was confirmed, but users need execution safeguards.
